ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not have a Romano-specific code; 102263004 (Cheese substance) used as closest parent anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Romano cheese is approximately 10 g (about 1 tablespoon grated), commonly used as a flavouring or topping on pasta, salads, and soups.

nutrient_source.title: Romano Cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 01037)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Romano cheese used as primary nutrient anchor. HPB Singapore database values (energy 387 kcal, protein 31.8 g, fat 26.94 g, carbohydrate 3.63 g, sodium 1433 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy and are retained. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and additional micronutrients sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01037.

gi_source.title: N/A — Romano cheese contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable for hard aged cheeses
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hard aged cheeses such as Romano are not listed in the University of Sydney GI Database because their carbohydrate content is negligible (3.63 g/100 g) and they produce no meaningful glycaemic response. GI is classified as not applicable per established nutritional convention for hard cheeses. Glycemic load per 10 g serving is 0.0 (carbohydrate per serving = 0.36 g, effectively zero GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1433 mg/100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Romano cheese is strongly contraindicated as a regular ingredient for individuals with hypertension; restrict use to a trace garnish of 5–10 g per dish and monitor total daily sodium intake against the 2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.63 g/100 g) and low sugar (0.73 g/100 g) produce minimal glycaemic impact, but high saturated fat (17.12 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular large portions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Romano cheese is acceptable in small amounts (10–15 g) as a flavouring for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its negligible glycaemic impact, but portions should be limited to avoid saturated fat-driven insulin resistance; avoid pairing with high-GI refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (17.12 g/100 g), trans fat (0.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (104 mg/100 g), and sodium (1433 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Romano cheese to occasional trace use (5–10 g) as a flavouring only, preferring lower-fat, lower-sodium cheese alternatives such as part-skim ricotta or fresh mozzarella to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (17.12 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium (1433 mg/100 g) worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Romano cheese should be used only as a trace garnish (5 g or less) for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high calcium content (1064 mg/100 g) is beneficial for bone protection but can be obtained from lower-fat, lower-sodium dairy sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Romano cheese is a hard, dry, intensely flavoured Italian-style aged cheese traditionally produced from sheep's milk (Pecorino Romano) or cow's milk (Vacchino Romano), originating from the Lazio region of Italy. It undergoes a lengthy salting and ripening process — typically 5 to 12 months — which produces its characteristic sharp, piquant, and salty flavour profile with a granular, crumbly texture ideal for grating. Romano is most commonly used as a finishing cheese grated over pasta dishes, soups, salads, and risottos, and is a key ingredient in classic Italian-American recipes such as pasta e fagioli and Caesar salad dressing. Nutritionally, Romano cheese is very high in protein (31.8 g/100 g), high in fat (26.94 g/100 g, of which 17.12 g is saturated), and exceptionally high in calcium (1064 mg/100 g) and sodium (1433 mg/100 g). Its carbohydrate content is negligible (3.63 g/100 g), making it glycaemically inert. Clinically, the very high sodium content makes Romano cheese strongly contraindicated for hypertension management, and its saturated fat and trans fat content raise cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. It is best used in small amounts (5–15 g) as a flavour-enhancing garnish rather than a primary ingredient. Romano cheese is gluten-free and suitable for ketogenic dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01037 for Romano cheese — an exact match. Saturated fat, fatty acid profile, and micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. No estimation or analogy required for macronutrients.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Romano cheese is an animal-derived dairy product containing no plant fibre. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g — naturally zero for an aged animal-derived cheese product. Trans fat 0.3 g/100 g is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid precursors) reported in USDA SR Legacy; this is distinct from industrially produced trans fat but is still noted as pro-inflammatory at elevated intake. Romano cheese; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (3.63 g/100 g); GI classification set to not_applicable per established nutritional convention for hard aged cheeses. Halal status is not_certified — Romano cheese may use animal rennet of non-halal origin; consumers should verify certification. Kosher status similarly depends on rennet source and supervision. The input record listed trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g in the rheumatoid arthritis health note; this value has been incorporated into per_100g.trans_fat_g based on USDA SR Legacy data for Romano cheese.
